name: Coyolxauhqui
description: Coyolxauhqui, whose name translates to "Golden Bells" or "She Who is Adorned with Bells on Her Face," is a significant lunar deity in Aztec mythology. She is primarily known for her role in the myth of her violent dismemberment by her brother, Huitzilopochtli, the god of war. This myth, prominently displayed on the Great Temple (Templo Mayor) in Tenochtitlan, underscores themes of matricide, sibling rivalry, and the establishment of patriarchal dominance within the Aztec pantheon. Coyolxauhqui's story is not merely a tale of defeat, but also a representation of the cyclical nature of the moon, its phases of waxing and waning mirroring her dismemberment and potential for rebirth. She is often depicted with bells on her cheeks, signifying her lunar association, and her defeat is interpreted as the triumph of the sun (Huitzilopochtli) over the moon, representing the daily victory of light over darkness. Her dismembered body, scattered across the Coatepec mountain, symbolizes the scattering of moonlight across the night sky and the cyclical nature of existence.
subtitle: Moon Goddess of Dismemberment
titles: She Who is Adorned with Bells on Her Face

keyMyths.title: The Birth of Huitzilopochtli and the Dismemberment of Coyolxauhqui
keyMyths.description: Coatlicue, Coyolxauhqui's mother, became pregnant in a miraculous way, causing Coyolxauhqui and her 400 brothers to become enraged, believing she had dishonored them. They plotted to kill her, but Huitzilopochtli was born fully armed and immediately defended his mother, slaying Coyolxauhqui and scattering her dismembered body across the Coatepec mountain. This act established Huitzilopochtli's dominance and solidified the new cosmic order.
keyMyths.source: Sahagún, Bernardino de. *General History of the Things of New Spain*.

keyMyths.title: Coyolxauhqui's Rebellion
keyMyths.description: Coyolxauhqui's rebellion against her mother's mysterious pregnancy can be interpreted as a challenge to the established order and a defense of traditional values. Her anger stemmed from a perceived threat to her status and the honor of her family. This rebellion, though ultimately unsuccessful, highlights the tensions between tradition and change within Aztec society and the power dynamics within the family.

keyMyths.title: The Representation on the Templo Mayor
keyMyths.description: The large stone disk depicting Coyolxauhqui's dismembered body, discovered at the base of the Templo Mayor, served as a powerful reminder of Huitzilopochtli's victory and the consequences of challenging the established order. It was likely used during sacrificial rituals, symbolically reenacting the myth and reinforcing the Aztec worldview. The placement of the disk at the foot of the temple emphasized the subjugation of the lunar deity to the solar deity.
keyMyths.source: Matos Moctezuma, Eduardo. *The Great Temple of the Aztecs: Treasure of Tenochtitlan*.
